coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots modified TiO2 composites for hydrogen production and selective glucose photoreforming
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Science Press and Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by ELSEVIER B.V. and Science Press. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Science Press and Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by ELSEVIER B.V. and Science Press.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Heng
Yu, Xinti
Li, Chao-Fan
Yu, Wenbei
Wang, Aiguo
Hu, Zhi-Yi
Larter, Steve
Li, Yu
Golam Kibria, Md
Hu, Jinguang
coredata.dc:description:
Lignocellulosic biomass photoreforming is a promising and alternative strategy for both sustainable H2 production and biomass valorization with infinite solar energy. However, harsh reaction conditions (high alkalinity or toxic organic solvents), with low biomass conversion and selectivity are often reported in literature. In this work, we report glucose photoreforming for coproduction of H2 and arabinose with improved selectivity under neutral condition using carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified TiO2 composites. We show that the conventional CQDs fabricated by a facile one-step hydrothermal process could be endowed with novel color changing property, due to the particle aggregation under the regulation of incident light. The as-fabricated CQDs/TiO2 composites with certain colored CQDs could greatly improve glucose to arabinose conversion selectivity (~75%) together with efficient hydrogen evolution (up to 2.43 mmolh−1g−1) in water. The arabinose is produced via the direct C1-C2 α-scissions mechanism with reactive oxygen species of
O2 − and
OH, as evidenced by 13C labeled glucose and the electron spin-resonance (ESR) studies, respectively. This work not only sheds new lights on CQDs assisted photobiorefinery for biomass valorization and H2 coproduction, but also opens the door for rationale design of different colored CQDs and their potential applications for solar energy utilization in the noble-metal-free system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CQDs
Size regulation
Hydrogen production
Glucose photoreforming
Arabinose

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photocatalytic water splitting for H2 evolution

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthetic scheme of CQDs by hydrothermal treatment and subsequent light regulation with 1 h and 2 h from (a) sodium citrate and (b) citric acid with corresponding photographs. (c) FTIR spectrum, XPS high-resolution (d) C 1s and (e) N 1s spectra of CQDs-Na and CQDs-H.
TEM and HR-TEM images of (a-d) CQDs-Na and (e-h) CQDs-H with different colors. Insets are the SAED and corresponding size distribution.

UV–vis absorption of the fabricated (a) CQDs-Na and (b) CQDs-H with different color (Insets are the corresponding photographs). PL emission spectra and quantum yields of (c, e) CQDs-Na and (d, f) CQDs-H at 365 nm excitation wavelength.

Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activities in water of (a) P25-CQDs-Na, and (b) P25-CQDs-H; (c) calculated H2 evolution rates; (d) long-time recycling test of P25-CQDs-Na-2 and P25-CQDs-H-2; PL spectra of P25-CQDs composites fabricated by CQDs-Na (e) and CQDs-H (f) with different color.
(a) Gas-phase products from glucose photoreforming, (b) glucose conversion, (c) arabinose selectivity and (d) calculated carbon balance.

(a) DMPO ESR spin-trapping for
O2 − (A: under dark condition, B: without oxygen) and (b)
OH (A: under dark condition). (c) The effect of different scavengers on glucose conversion, (d) 13C NMR for glucose-1-13C before and after reaction and (e) proposed reaction mechanism for glucose photoreforming.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots modified TiO2 composites for hydrogen production and selective glucose photoreforming

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanostructure Research Centre, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan 430070, Hubei, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Geosciences, University of Calgary, 2500 University Drive, NW, Calgary, Alberta T2N 1N4, Canada

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contribute equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The color changeable CQDs with TiO2 composites exhibit excellent H2 generation activity and high arabinose selectivity from glucose photoreforming.

Lignocellulosic biomass photoreforming is a promising and alternative strategy for both sustainable H2 production and biomass valorization with infinite solar energy. However, harsh reaction conditions (high alkalinity or toxic organic solvents), with low biomass conversion and selectivity are often reported in literature. In this work, we report glucose photoreforming for coproduction of H2 and arabinose with improved selectivity under neutral condition using carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified TiO2 composites. We show that the conventional CQDs fabricated by a facile one-step hydrothermal process could be endowed with novel color changing property, due to the particle aggregation under the regulation of incident light. The as-fabricated CQDs/TiO2 composites with certain colored CQDs could greatly improve glucose to arabinose conversion selectivity (~75%) together with efficient hydrogen evolution (up to 2.43 mmolh−1g−1) in water. The arabinose is produced via the direct C1-C2 α-scissions mechanism with reactive oxygen species of
O2 − and
OH, as evidenced by 13C labeled glucose and the electron spin-resonance (ESR) studies, respectively. This work not only sheds new lights on CQDs assisted photobiorefinery for biomass valorization and H2 coproduction, but also opens the door for rationale design of different colored CQDs and their potential applications for solar energy utilization in the noble-metal-free system.

ce:section.ce:para: Traditional biomass biorefinery platforms such as thermochemical and biological processes are capital and carbon-intensive, and consequently not economically and environmentally attractive at current stage [1,2]. In this context, there has been growing momentum on solar-driven biomass conversion, the so-called “biomass photoreforming”. Photoreforming of biomass or biomass-derived components not only allows biomass valorization to produce value-added chemicals, but also offers a feasible approach for sustainable H2 coproduction [3–5]. To realize this concept, the photocatalytic conversion pathway of biomass building block chemicals should be elucidated. Glucose, the basic unit of cellulose that accounts for about 40–60 wt% of lignocellulosic biomass in nature, has been widely selected to demonstrate this biomass photoreforming concept [6]. However, current glucose photoreforming still suffers from low conversion and/or selectivity at aqueous environment. To overcome these challenges, rational photocatalyst design with multiple functions to enhance glucose conversion efficacy and selectivity is needed.

An ideal photocatalyst for glucose photoreforming requires good charge separation for efficient hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), as well as strong interaction with glucose for selective oxidation. Among various potential photocatalysts, TiO2 stands out since the unsaturated titanium atoms or oxygen vacancy on the surface could directly interact with the hydroxyl oxygen atoms of glucose to form the ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT) complex for efficient electron transfer to reduce the unselective radical based oxidation reaction [7,8]. However, co-catalysts are often needed to improve the separation efficiency of photogenerated electrons and holes of TiO2. The most commonly used co-catalysts are noble metals such as Pt, Au, Ag et al., due to their large work function [9]. But the high prices and rare content of these noble metals heavily hinder their practical applications. Herein, developing low-cost, efficient and noble metal-free photocatalysts has drawn particular attention [10–12].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), an emerging carbon nanomaterial, has captured great attention in recent years. Due to their excellent optic properties and high electrical conductivity, CQDs can be utilized as photosensitizer and/or co-catalysts for photocatalytic applications [13–15]. Although CQDs have been employed as co-catalyst for HER during water splitting [16–19], their roles for biomass photoreforming have yet to be explored. In addition, previous works have only focused on regulating the CQDs composition (e.g. introducing heteroatoms) to improve the charge separation, but the relationship between the CQDs size and photocatalytic activity is not reported to the best of our knowledge [20,21]. As changing co-catalyst size has been reported to have great effect on charge separation at the contact interface, it herein provides a guidance to investigate the CQDs size on photocatalytic activity [22].

To fill these gaps, various sizes and compositions of CQDs were fabricated and their roles as co-catalysts for glucose photoreforming were investigated in this study. The obtained CQDs showed abundant doping states, surface functional groups, and sensitive color changeable property. We found different carbon sources endowed CQDs with different colors, and the incident light could regulate the size of CQDs and thus, their color changing property. Interestingly, although the photoluminescent quantum yield was reduced along with increasing the size of CQDs, the corresponding CQDs/TiO2 composites exhibited much enhanced H2 evolution activity due to the enhanced ability for charge separation. When the screened CQDs/TiO2 composites were used for glucose photoreforming, arabinose (a high value sugar) was selectively produced (~75%) in water through decarboxylation reaction from C1-C2 α-scissions, along with reasonable amount of gas fuels (H2 and CO) coproduction. This work not only provides a promising strategy for precisely regulating the size of CQDs through a facile process, but also sheds some light on the development of noble-metal free photocatalysts for biomass photoreforming.

All the reagents in this experiment are of analytic grade and commercially available. Citrate acid, sodium citrate, ammonium bicarbonate, TiO2 (P25) and D-glucose were purchased from Aldrich without further purification.

CQDs were synthesized via bottom-up strategy using different citrate as carbon source according to previous report with some modification [23]. Briefly, citrate (1.0 g sodium citrate or 0.65 g citrate acid), 7.5 g NH4HCO3 and 100 ml deionized water were mixed with stirring. The obtained transparent solution was sealed into a Teflon-lined autoclave for hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 4 h. After reaction, the autoclave was fast cooled to room temperature with water. Finally, the CQDs solution was purified through a dialysis tube (1000 Da, molecular weight cutoff) for about 24 h in dark and pale-yellow solution was collected. The size of CQDs was regulated by irradiation with simulated sunlight for different time. The obtained solution with different color was freeze-dried for further use.

To synthesize CQDs/TiO2 composites, 0.5 g P25 was added into 50 mL CQDs solution. The mixture was kept stirring for 2 h at room temperature. Subsequently, the suspension was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave for hydrothermal treatment at 140 °C for 4 h. The resulting CQDs/TiO2 composites were obtained after filtration, washed with deionized water and dried at 60 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic water splitting for H2 evolution
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic reactions were carried out in a closed circulation system (Labsolar-6A, Beijing Perfectlight Technology Co., Ltd) using a PLS-SXE-300 lamp. 20 mg CQDs/TiO2 composite was immersed in 100 mL aqueous solution containing 50 mL methanol as the sacrificial agents. The mixture was sealed in a quartz vessel and stirred during photoreaction. After degassing, the vessel was under irradiation of UV–vis light (320–780 nm, 2.1 W/cm2). The gas products were analyzed periodically by an Agilent 7890A gas chromatograph (GC) with a thermal conductivity detector (TCD). The 13C nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra were recorded on a Bruker Advance III 400 spectrometer with a BBFO probe.

10 mg photocatalyst was dispersed in the 25 mL 2 g/L glucose solution and the suspension was treated with sonication for 0.5 h before irradiation by a 300 W Xenon lamp. The gas products were periodically quantified by a GC while the liquid products were analysed by a high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC, 1200 Agilent system) equipped with a refractive index detector (RID) and a photodiode array detector (DAD). The column was Hi-Plex H (6.5 × 300 mm, 8 µm, Agilent) and 0.005 M H2SO4 was used as an eluting solvent with a flow rate of 0.6 mL/min. Glucose conversion, arabinose selectivity and carbon balance were calculated as follows:
Glucose conversion (%) = ((Glucose)in − (Glucose)out)/(Glucose)in
Arabinose selectivity (%) = (Arabinose)out/((Glucose)in-(Glucose)out)
Carbon balance (%) = Carbon in products/carbon in converted glucose
Selectivity and carbon balance values were calculated on a molar basis. Carbon in both liquid-phase and gas-phase products were considered.

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) was determined according to a procedure reported elsewhere [24,25]. Rhodamine 6G in ethanol (95%) was chosen as the standard reference and the QY of samples were calculated by following equation:
Q
C Q D s
=
Q R
∗
F
C Q D s
F R
∗
A R
A
C Q D s
∗
η
C Q D s
2
η
R
2
where, Q is the quantum yield, F represents the fluorescence intensity, A is the absorbance under 365 nm light and η represents the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript R refers to the reference. As the concentration of samples is extremely low, the ratio η CQDs/η R is equal to 1 due to the extremely similar refractive index for ethanol (1.36) and water (1.33).

The crystalline phase analysis of the as-fabricated composites was examined by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, D8 ADVANCE) at 40 kV, 40 mA equipped with a Cu anode X-ray tube (Cu Kα X-rays, λ = 1.54056 Å) at a scan rate of 0.05° s−1. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) is acquired on the copper microgrids with the honey carbon film using a Thermo Fisher Scientific Talos F200S microscope. The UV–Vis absorption spectra are collected by SHIMADZU UV–Vis spectrophotometer in the range of 200–800 nm with BaSO4 as the reference. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra are recorded using fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer LS-55). For CQDs solution, the excitation slit width was 10 nm and emission slit width was 12 nm. For CQDs/TiO2 composites, the excitation slit width was 12 nm and emission slit width was 6 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed with a customized X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo fisher, Alpha equipped with a monochromatic Al Kα source). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet 6700 spectrometer using KBr tableting method in the range of 4000–400 cm−1. Electron spin-resonance (ESR) spectroscopy was used to study molecules and materials with unpaired electrons and the 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) was chosen as a spin trap for the detection of hydroxyl radical (
OH) and superoxide (
O2 −). The 13C nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra were recorded on a Bruker Advance III 400 spectrometer with a BBFO probe and all samples were in same concentration.

Fig. 1 a and b show the schematic diagram of CQDs synthesis from sodium citrate and citric acid, respectively. The hydrothermal treatment provides high temperature and the decomposition of ammonium bicarbonate provides high pressure, which synergistically promote the aggregation of the small precursors to CQDs. These obtained CQDs are endowed with interesting color changeable properties. With the regulation of incident light, the as-fabricated CQDs from sodium citrate gradually change from bright yellow (CQDs-Na) to orange (CQDs-Na-1), then to red (CQDs-Na-2) (Fig. 1a). On the other hand, the CQDs prepared from citrate acid gradually change from bright yellow (CQDs-H) to light green (CQDs-H-1), then to dark green (CQDs-H-2) (Fig. 1b). The components and sizes of these CQDs are speculated to correspond to their color changing properties.

The functional groups of CQDs were then assessed by FTIR and XPS, respectively. The characteristic stretching vibrations of C=O, C-N and epoxide species indicate the presence of polymerization reaction (with nitrogen involved) during the CQDs formation from both precursors (Fig. 1c) [26,27]. As expected, the bending vibration intensity of C-N at around 1257 cm−1 in CQDs-H is much sharper than that in CQDs-Na, indicating the greatly improved nitrogen content in CQDs-H. To confirm the increased content of nitrogen in CQDs-H, XPS analysis was further conducted. The detected C-N bond for CQDs indicates the presence of nitrogen doping and the dopant nitrogen content is sparse according to the negligible intensity of N1s in CQDs-Na (Figs. 1d and S1a). The nitrogen content greatly increases in the CQDs-H as confirmed by the increased N1s peak in Fig. S1b. In this case, ammonium ion not only balances the charge equilibriums but also reacts with the citrate to form the CQDs-H with high content of nitrogen dopant. The presence of nitrogen mainly exists in pyrrolic and graphitic states (Fig. 1e). The enhanced signal-to-noise ratio of N1s in CQDs-H compared with that in CQDs-Na also confirmed the increased nitrogen content in CQDs-H (Fig. 1e). The FTIR spectra were almost identical for each type of CQDs irradiated with different time, which indicates their color changes were likely caused by some physical changes such as particle size (Fig. S2).

The particle sizes of different CQDs were then investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) to further illustrate this unique color changing property. All the CQDs were monodispersed and the initial size of CQDs-Na (4.25 nm) was larger than that of CQDs-H (2.70 nm) (Fig. 2 a and e). The as-prepared CQDs showed good crystallinity and the lattice fringe, which could be clearly identified from the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) results (Fig. 2b and f). The crystallization characteristics of different CQDs were also revealed by XRD and compared with their precursor of sodium citrate and citric acid. Both precursors showed typical peaks which almost disappeared in their derived CQDs, indicating they were decomposed into small carbon nanoparticle under high temperature and high pressure (Fig. S3) [23]. All the CQDs exhibited a broad peak centered at around 30° which is similar to that of bulk graphite, indicating good crystallization characteristics. (Fig. S3). The particle sizes of CQDs were continuously increased under light irradiation, where the average size of CQDs-Na and CQDs-H increased from initial 4.25 nm and 2.70 nm to 7.00 nm and 4.75 nm, respectively. The increase of size was attributed to the aggregation of CQDs triggered by incident light and also endowed the CQDs with unique color changing property (Fig. 1a and b). Control experiment under dark condition further revealed the effect of irradiation on size change of CQDs (Fig. S4).

To better understand the light absorbance of different CQDs and the internal factor for size change, the intrinsic light absorption spectra of the as-fabricated CQDs were recorded in the range of 300–800 nm (Fig. 3 a and b). The strong absorption under 400 nm of CQDs-Na was attributed to the intrinsic π-π* (C=C) and n-π* (C=O) transition absorption, while the shoulder peak at 480 nm corresponds to cyclic structure (Fig. 3a) [28,29]. The decomposition of CQDs precursors i.e., sodium citrate and citric acid would produce small carbon nanoparticles which grew gradually to form CQDs in the decomposition process [23]. The growth could also produce cyclic structure similar to that of graphite but with doped nitrogen from ammonium bicarbonate [30,31]. Interestingly, the absorbance of this cyclic structure gradually increased under light irradiation, leading to the red color tendency of the CQDs-Na solution (Fig. 3a inset). This indicated that the incident light could regulate the assembly of CQDs, resulting in the growth of the cyclic structure. As for the CQDs-H, its absorption edge red-shifted to around 420 nm as compared with CQDs-Na with a shoulder peak located at around 640 nm (Fig. 3b), which was attributed to the doped nitrogen [30]. Similarly, the absorbance of CQDs-H solution in the whole visible light region gradually increased, leading to the green color tendency (Fig. 3b inset).

Fig. 3c and 3d show the emission spectra of CQDs-Na and CQDs-H under 365 nm excitation wavelength. These two kinds of CQDs all exhibited blue fluorescence with relatively large emission region. It should be noted that the original precursors (sodium citrate and citrate) are non-emissive in the visible region upon excitation at 365 nm. This indicates the blue fluorescence was generated by the as-fabricated CQDs [23]. The full width at half maximum of CQDs-Na was ~66 nm (Fig. 3c), similar to that of the reported F-CNPs [23]. The quantum yield was calculated to be about 54% (Fig. 3e) using rhodamine 6G in ethanol as the standard reagent [32]. Accompanied with the color changes of CQDs-Na under irradiation, the PL intensity rapidly reduced with small blue shift and the corresponding quantum yields reduced to 25% and 19% of CQDs-Na-1 and CQDs-Na-2 respectively (Fig. 3e). The CQDs-H showed relatively stronger fluorescent intensity and larger emission region (Fig. 3d). The main emission peak of CQDs-H located at around 456 nm with a 13 nm red shift compared with the main emission peak of CQDs-Na (Fig. 3f), which was ascribed to the improved content of nitrogen dopant [33]. Similarly, the whole PL intensity sharply dropped with the corresponding quantum yields of 37% and 24% for CQDs-H-1 and CQDs-H-2 respectively (Fig. 3f).

The color changing CQDs were then combined with commercial P25 for photocatalytic applications to investigate the relation between their color changing property and photocatalytic activity. Although the crystal phases and corresponding crystallinity of P25 were revealed to be the same with/without CQDs (Figs. S5a and S5b), all P25- CQDs nanocomposites showed an enhanced absorption in the whole visible light region, along with the red shifts of the absorption edges (Figs. S5c and S5d). The CQDs were also revealed to distribute on the surface of TiO2 evenly (Figs. S6 and S7). In order to evaluate P25-CQDs nanocomposites for glucose photoreforming, we firstly screened all the P25-CQDs composites via half reaction (hydrogen evolution reaction) from water splitting. Although the presence of inner heterojunction structure formed between anatase and rutile phases could help the separation of photogenerated charge carriers [34–36], the P25 still showed poor photocatalytic ability for H2 evolution (0.20 mmolh−1g−1) (Fig. 4 a and b). The P25-CQDs nanocomposites showed significantly enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen evolution performance, wherein the rates of H2 evolution of P25-CQDs-Na and P25-CQDs-H reached 0.81 mmolh−1g−1 and 1.42 mmolh−1g−1, 4-fold and 7-fold higher than pure P25, respectively (Fig. 4c). This enhancement was ascribed to the high electrical conductivity of CQDs, leading to the fast separation of electrons and holes. Comparing the CQDs from different precursors, P25-CQDs-H showed better HER performance than P25-CQDs-Na (Fig. 4c). This is likely due to the increased nitrogen content in CQDs-H, since the nitrogen doped CQDs demonstrated better charge separation efficiency in previous studies [37–39]. As for same CQDs with different sizes/colors, it appeared that the bigger the size of CQDs, the better H2 evolution performance of P25-CQDs (Fig. 4c). The H2 evolution rates for P25-CQDs-Na-2 and P25-CQDs-H-2 increased to 2.10 mmolh−1g−1 and 2.43 mmolh−1g−1, which were considerably higher than other P25-CQDs composites. Biomass photoreforming is an emerging research area, and to the best of our knowledge, this is the first study to use carbon dots for biomass photoreforming. Our photocatalysts with carbon dots also demonstrated the state-of-art performance for hydrogen production as compared with others in the literatures (Table S1). It was speculated that as the size increased, the widened ring structure of CQDs could greatly enhance the separation of electrons and holes. Steady PL was conducted to further confirm the enhanced charge separation in presence of CQDs (Fig. 4e and f). After decoration of CQDs, P25-CQDs composites showed much reduced fluorescence intensity which was generated during the recombination process of photogenerated electrons and holes, indicating that the introduction of CQDs indeed improved the charge separation efficiency. In addition, the two best photocatalysts have kept similar photocatalytic H2 evolution activities even after 20 h irradiation, indicating their excellent photostability (Fig. 4d), which was attributed to the stability in crystallinity and morphology of the as-prepared photocatalysts (Figs. S8 and S9). The apparent color of CQDs kept almost the same after longer irradiation time: red for CQDs from sodium citrate (CQDs-Na-3 and CQDs-Na-4) and dark green for CQDs from citric acid (CQDs-H-3 and CQDs-H-4) (Fig. S10). The corresponding composites showed sharp reduction in H2 evolution activities (Fig. S11). This is likely due to the further enlarged size of CQDs, leading to the obviously enhanced electrochemical impedance and reducing the separation efficiency of electrons and holes (Fig. S12). The increased particle size of CQDs with longer irradiation time was revealed by TEM and UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. S13).

After photocatalyst screening, glucose photoreforming was further carried out with the two best composites P25-CQDs-Na-2 and P25-CQDs-H-2. Gas products of H2 and CO were detected from glucose photoreforming (Fig. 5 a). The results demonstrated that the presence of CQDs significantly improved H2 production while had negligible effect on CO production. The improved H2 production was expected due to the enhanced separation efficiency of photogenerated electrons and holes by CQDs. While the CO generation could possibly come from the dehydration reaction of formic acid - a product from glucose photoreforming - which does not involve electrons and/or holes. Control experiments were performed by directly using CQDs in glucose solution or DI water without glucose. CO and H2 were detected in presence of glucose while negligible CO was detected in absence of glucose, indicating that the produced CO was indeed mainly from glucose photoreforming rather than CQDs (Fig. S14). It is worth noting that no CO2 was detected in the gas phase, which indicated that no over-oxidation reaction of glucose during photoreforming.

Pure TiO2 has been reported to have the ability to directly convert glucose under irradiation through the ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT) mechanism [8,40]. P25 showed about 12.2% conversion of glucose after 5 h photocatalytic reaction while the P25-CQDs composites exhibited much higher glucose conversion efficiency. The P25-CQDs-Na-2 and P25-CQDs-Na-2 reached approximately 27% of glucose conversion after 5 hours, which was about 2.2 times improvement as compared with pristine P25. These results indicated that the efficient charge separation achieved by CQDs not only improved H2 generation (Fig. 4a and b), but also significantly boosted the kinetics of glucose oxidation (Fig. 5b). Cycling test by recovering photocatalyst after reaction revealed the good photostability for glucose conversion (Fig. S15). Besides, the glucose conversion was also proven to be consistent with irradiation intensity (Fig. S16). The products from glucose conversion were distributed as arabinose, erythrose, glycolic acid, dihydroxyacetone, formic acid with arabinose as the primary product (Table S1). The as-fabricated composites with CQDs showed much better arabinose production and selectivity (76.1% for P25-CQDs-Na-2 and 74.4% for P25-CQDs-H-2) than pristine P25 (Fig. 5c and d). The carbon balance during the photocatalytic reaction gradually reduced (from ~ 95% after 1 h to ~ 70% after 5 h) as some intermediate chemicals with low concentration could not be quantified (Fig. S17).

We next investigate the potential active species (
O2 − and
OH) in the photocatalytic system by electron spin-resonance (ESR). This will help us to better understand the mechanism of glucose photoreforming. The ESR measurement was carried out with 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as a spin trap which could react with
O2 − to produce DMPO/
O2 −, resulting the corresponding signal. No signals were observed for dark condition and without oxygen while typical ESR signal for
O2 − was observed with light and oxygen, indicating the production of
O2 − with P25-CQDs under light irradiation (Fig. 6 a). On the other hand, DMPO/
OH with typically relative intensity of 1:2:2:1 was also observed under irradiation (Fig. 6b). To further clarify the effects of
O2 − and
OH on glucose photocatalytic conversion, scavengers of benzoquinone (BQ) and isopropanol (IPA) were separately added into the reaction system to consume
O2 − and
OH, respectively. The results demonstrated that both
O2 − and
OH facilitated glucose conversion while
O2 − played the dominant effect (Fig. 6c). 13C NMR was also conducted to better elucidate the reaction pathway from glucose to arabinose, using 13C labeled glucose in the C1 position. It appeared that the signal intensities of α-C1 and β-C1 labeled by 13C significantly decreased and another two peaks of formate and carbonate emerged after reaction (Fig. 6d) [41–43]. Herein, we conclude that glucose is reformed by reactive oxygen species to arabinose with the C1-C2 α-scissions mechanism.

Based on the above experimental results, we proposed the photocatalytic mechanism for glucose conversion with gases coproduction (Fig. 6e). Under light irradiation, electrons at the valence band are excited into the conduction band with holes left at the valence band. The photogenerated electrons are partially trapped by CQDs and then trigger proton reduction to produce H2 while the residual electrons react with absorbed oxygen to produce
O2 −. The photogenerated holes react with absorbed water to produce
OH. Previous investigation indicates that glucose reacts with these reactive oxygen species to produce gluconic acid through oxygen transfer reaction [44]. Then arabinose is finally produced after decarboxylation from C1-C2 α-scissions with formic acid formation and formic acid is subsequently reacted through dehydration process to produce CO [45].

In summary, blue-fluorescent carbon quantum dots have been successfully fabricated through a facile one-step hydrothermal strategy using citrates as carbon source. The prepared CQDs were endowed with color changing properties. Briefly, CQDs prepared from sodium citrate gradually changed to red while CQDs fabricated by citric acid turned to green after light irradiation. The incident light can regulate the particle size of CQDs by inducing their aggregation and corresponding P25-CQDs composites exhibited much enhanced photocatalytic H2 evolution ability due to the enhanced charge separation efficiency. The highest performance was achieved from red CQDs (2.10 mmolh−1g−1 for P25-CQDs-Na-2) and dark green CQDs (2.43 mmolh−1g−1 for P25-CQDs-H-2). The P25-CQDs composites demonstrated good glucose conversion and reactive oxygen species of
O2 − and
OH were responsible for glucose activation and conversion. Arabinose was selectively (~75%) produced through the decarboxylation reaction from C1-C2 α-scissions. Our work here demonstrates a facile and novel strategy to regulate the particle size of CQDs for noble-metal free photocatalysis. The color changing property induced by incident light could also endow CQDs with wide application in light-emitting diodes.
